title: Influence of critical parameters of nanosuspension formulation on the permeability of a poorly soluble drug through the skin--a case study.

anchor_evidence.locator: Block 64: "the skin was mounted on static vertical Franz Diffusion cells—Permegear Inc., Bethlehem, PA (receptor volume 5.1 ml, donor area 0.64 cm²)"
anchor_evidence.snippet: Block 64: "the skin was mounted on static vertical Franz Diffusion cells—Permegear Inc., Bethlehem, PA (receptor volume 5.1 ml, donor area 0.64 cm²)"

anchor_evidence.locator: Table II (DOE cumulative permeation at 24, 48, 72 h and flux values); Fig. 2 (cumulative permeation plot, pig skin, multiple polymer formulations); Fig. 4/Block 117 caption (cumulative permeation through human skin from optimised formulation, n=3)
anchor_evidence.snippet: The cumulative amount of drug permeated through the skin after 24, 48, and 72 h is shown in Table II. Flux values were determined for each permeation study (Table II).
As shown in Table I, several formulations were evaluated using nanosuspensions... We used different types of gel forming polymers at three different concentrations (Table I).

notes: Primary IVPT study using ibuprofen as model drug in nanosuspension gel formulations. Barriers: dermatomed porcine skin (DOE study) and human skin (optimised formulation confirmatory run). Franz diffusion cells explicitly named (Permegear, 5.1 mL receptor, 0.64 cm² donor area, 37°C, 600 RPM, PBS pH 7.4 receptor). Key endpoints: cumulative amount permeated (μg/cm²) at 4, 8, 12, 24, 36, 48, 72 h and flux (μg/cm²/h) — both reported in Table II and figures. Formulation details (% drug, TPGS, HPMC variants, particle size) in Table I and Table II. DOE 2³ factorial design with HPMC K100 concentration, vitamin E TPGS level, and particle size as factors. Flux range reported: e.g. F2=29.7, F4=19.3 μg/cm²/h. Sink condition confirmed (saturation solubility 1.6 mg/mL at pH 7.4, 37°C). All primary data appear to be in main-text tables and figures; no supplementary material indicated.
